access.entityLabel: General Corpus source XML for HenFabl

entities.description: The DLPSTEXTCLASS XML artifact consumed by this repository's publication workflow and supplied through the Kyvero-Press/CME git submodule.
entities.label: General Corpus source XML for HenFabl
notes: The XML contains 14 DIV1, 15 DIV2, 424 LG, 2,975 L, 109 PB, and 30 HEAD elements; two numbered lines sit directly outside LG and one seven-line stanza is unnumbered.
Its 109 page markers are Smith's odd Harleian pages 3-219, not continuous pagination of the whole parallel-text edition.

rights.basis: The work was created and circulated centuries before modern copyright terms.
rights.component: underlying late-medieval literary work
notes: This conclusion does not transfer automatically to modern encodings, editorial matter, scans, or provider terms.

entities.description: A legacy COCOA-marked, uppercase machine-readable text made for Older Scottish Textual Archive and Dictionary of the Older Scottish Tongue work, deposited in 1984 and now distributed with a TEI header.
entities.label: Oxford Text Archive item 0243 / henfable-0243
notes: The header says the printed source contains five versions and warns that the electronic text may have been constructed from all five, but staff could not investigate.
The text's N code is BASS PR and its opening title is Bassandyne 1571; those labels do not by themselves prove direct transcription from the physical Bassandyne copy.

entities.description: Smith's parallel presentation of the five early Fables streams, physically the publication's second volume and Scottish Text Society first series volume 55.

Charteris occupies pp. 2-218 left/even, Harleian pp. 3-219 right/odd, Makculloch pp. 223-228, Bannatyne pp. 231-315, and Asloan-Chalmers pp. 319-327.

notes: The main manuscript is associated with Magnus Makculloch; the vernacular additions may be in the hand of John Purde.

physicalDescription.extent: 375 leaves in the main manuscript object
notes: Compiled chiefly during the last three months of 1568; the Fables selection is in the fifth part.

workPortion.label: Three non-contiguous Henryson Fables runs in the fifth part
locators: NLS Adv.MS.1.1.6, vol. II, ff. 298r-302r; IIIF canvases 279-287
NLS Adv.MS.1.1.6, vol. II, ff. 310v-317v; IIIF canvases 304-318
NLS Adv.MS.1.1.6, vol. II, ff. 326v-342v; IIIF canvases 335-368
notes: The start and end URLs delimit the outer span only; the work is present in the three exact discontinuous runs above, with unrelated works between them.
Canvas 279 supplies the fifth-part heading; the first Fables narrative begins on canvas 281, and canvas 287 ends that run before The Howlat begins on the same leaf.
Canvas 335 begins the final run after the end of The Bludy Serk on the same leaf; canvas 368 ends the Lion and Mouse selection before the next work.

next_steps: Seek surviving OSTA/DOST production notes or a concordance-generation specification that identifies the base column and every supplied reading.
notes: The Bassandyne title, Harleian page markers, and Charteris-only unnumbered stanza are all directly observed and must remain visible until the construction is documented.
openQuestions.question: Exactly how was OTA 0243's one continuous BASS PR text constructed from Smith's five separately printed streams?

next_steps: Obtain an exact current British Library catalog citation before adding a shelfmark or claiming that no digitization exists internally.
openQuestions.question: What is the verified current British Library shelfmark, access route, and digital availability of the unique Charteris-Lekpreuik copy formerly at Britwell Court?

next_steps: Monitor the exact British Library record and request imaging or reuse information if an image-based comparison is required.
openQuestions.question: When will complete public images for Harley MS 3865 become available again?

next_steps: Ask U-M Digital Content and Collections for production documentation while retaining only directly observed transformation claims.
openQuestions.question: What item-level U-M production record documents the 1994 conversion from OTA COCOA text to the present DLPSTEXTCLASS structure and its local section labels?

reviewNotes: Print dates, manuscript dates, transcript dates, edition dates, encoding dates, repository identity, and facsimile dates are modeled as separate layers.

The full Bannatyne and Asloan caches represent their complete surviving provider objects; their Henryson portions are explicitly discontinuous or partial, and historical losses are not hidden by the word complete.
No complete object was silently reduced to work-only pages: the cached Bassandyne, Smith/Johnson, Bannatyne, and Asloan files retain their full physical or provider-defined source boundaries.
No direct Bassandyne-to-OTA relation is asserted. Bassandyne enters the supported chain through Harley's dependence, while OTA's internally inconsistent source labeling remains an open question.

lineage.summary: HenFabl is the General Corpus XML copy of a 1994 University of Michigan Humanities Text Initiative encoding supplied from Oxford Text Archive item 0243. The OTA header identifies G. Gregory Smith's 1906 parallel-text edition, pp. 2-327, as its printed source, while the plain text labels itself BASS PR, reproduces Thomas Bassandyne's 1571 title, follows Smith's Harleian odd-page sequence in the U-M XML, and includes at least one stanza present in the Charteris stream but absent from Harleian. The exact construction of that electronic text is therefore unresolved and is not reduced to a direct transcription of one physical source. Smith printed five early streams: the 1569/1570 Charteris edition, Harley MS 3865, Makculloch MS Laing III.149, Bannatyne MS Adv.MS.1.1.6, and William Gibb's 1810 transcript of the surviving Asloan Two Mice. Current scholarship identifies Harley MS 3865 as a copy of Bassandyne's 1571 print. Complete public source objects cached here comprise the unique Bassandyne print, the full Bannatyne main manuscript, the full Asloan manuscript, and the complete 1968 Johnson facsimile reprint of Smith's 1906 text volume.

catalogSummary.author: Robert Henryson
catalogSummary.editor: G. Gregory Smith (1906 source edition); George Stevenson transcribed and collated its five source streams
catalogSummary.region: Scotland; Henryson is associated with Dunfermline, but the exact place of composition is unproved
catalogSummary.title: The Morall Fabillis of Esope the Phrygian

catalogingSubject.label: The Morall Fabillis of Esope the Phrygian
catalogingSubject.scope_note: The late-medieval Middle Scots verse work conventionally attributed to Robert Henryson and represented as a 13-fable sequence with two prologues and a Moralitas after every narrative. Single_work treats that sequence as the present cataloging unit; it does not decide the scholarly debate over whether all 13 fables were conceived as one authorially ordered collection. The abstract work remains distinct from its early prints and manuscripts, Smith's five-stream 1906 edition, the OTA and U-M encodings, and the repository XML.

contentParts.label: General Prologue
contentParts.summary: A defense of morally useful fiction and a declaration of translation from Aesop into the mother tongue.

contentParts.label: The Cock and the Jasp
contentParts.summary: A cock finds a jewel but values food above a treasure he cannot use.

contentParts.label: Moralitas: The Cock and the Jasp
contentParts.summary: The jewel signifies prudence and learning, while the cock figures contempt for knowledge.

contentParts.label: The Uponland Mouse and the Burgess Mouse (The Two Mice)
contentParts.summary: Country and town mice test one another's ways of life and contrast humble security with anxious abundance.

contentParts.label: Moralitas: The Two Mice
contentParts.summary: The safer contentment of a simple life is preferred to wealth exposed to continual fear.

contentParts.label: Schir Chantecleir and the Fox
contentParts.summary: A fox captures a cock through flattery and loses him when tricked into speaking.

contentParts.label: Moralitas: Schir Chantecleir and the Fox

locators.value: HenFabl:4.1; numbered lines 586-606 plus one unnumbered seven-line stanza

notes: The unnumbered stanza beginning 'Fy puft vp pryde' is present in the Charteris column of Smith but absent from the facing Harleian column, exposing cross-stream supplementation in the electronic text.
contentParts.summary: The tale warns against flattery, pride, and heedless speech.

contentParts.label: The Fox's Confession to Friar Wolf Waitskaith
notes: Line 615 is a direct DIV1 child outside an LG, an encoding boundary anomaly rather than evidence of prose.
contentParts.summary: The fox confesses to a corrupt wolf friar whose counsel fails to reform appetite or predation.

contentParts.label: Moralitas: The Fox's Confession
contentParts.summary: The morality attacks hypocritical confession and corrupt spiritual guidance.

contentParts.label: The Trial of the Fox / Parliament of Four-footed Beasts
contentParts.summary: The fox's heir is summoned before the lion's parliament in an extended satire of law, court, and predatory power.

contentParts.label: Moralitas: The Trial of the Fox
contentParts.summary: The animal court is applied to human estates, false counsel, and institutional injustice.

contentParts.label: The Sheep and the Dog
contentParts.summary: A sheep loses a legal case to a dog through false witnesses and a corrupt court.

contentParts.label: Moralitas: The Sheep and the Dog
contentParts.summary: The morality condemns perverted law, bribery, oppression, and false testimony.

contentParts.label: Prologue to The Lion and the Mouse
contentParts.summary: A dream-vision encounter with Aesop introduces the following fable and reflects on poetic authority.

contentParts.label: The Lion and the Mouse
notes: The XML mislabels HenFabl:8.2 as a second prologue; its contents are the fable narrative.
contentParts.summary: A mouse spared by a lion later frees the lion from a net, demonstrating reciprocal mercy across unequal power.

contentParts.label: Moralitas: The Lion and the Mouse
contentParts.summary: The morality commends mercy and warns rulers not to despise the weak.

contentParts.label: The Preaching of the Swallow
contentParts.summary: A swallow's repeatedly ignored warnings about flax and snares culminate in the destruction of the other birds.

contentParts.label: Moralitas: The Preaching of the Swallow
contentParts.summary: The morality urges timely attention to wise teaching and spiritual warning.

contentParts.label: The Fox, the Wolf, and the Cadger's Nekhering
contentParts.summary: The fox tricks a wolf into pursuing a herring cart, and the wolf suffers for greed and credulity.

contentParts.label: Moralitas: The Nekhering
contentParts.summary: The morality exposes appetite, deception, and the hazards of corrupt companionship.

contentParts.label: The Fox, the Wolf, and the Shadow of the Moon
contentParts.summary: The fox persuades the hungry wolf that the moon's reflection is cheese and exploits his gullibility.

contentParts.label: Moralitas: The Shadow of the Moon
contentParts.summary: The morality warns against fantasy, greed, and reliance on deceitful advisers.

contentParts.label: The Wolf and the Wether
notes: Line 2475 is a direct DIV1 child outside an LG, an encoding boundary anomaly rather than prose.
contentParts.summary: A wether wearing a dog's skin briefly frightens a wolf before his disguise and weakness are exposed.

contentParts.label: Moralitas: The Wolf and the Wether
contentParts.summary: The morality attacks false appearance, presumption, and those who assume offices beyond their capacity.

contentParts.label: The Wolf and the Lamb
contentParts.summary: A wolf invents successive accusations against an innocent lamb and kills him despite reasoned legal defense.

contentParts.label: Moralitas: The Wolf and the Lamb
contentParts.summary: The lamb figures laborers and the poor, while wolves figure corrupt lawyers, lords, landlords, and extortioners.

contentParts.label: The Paddock and the Mouse
contentParts.summary: A deceitful frog binds herself to a mouse in crossing water; a kite seizes and kills both adversaries.

contentParts.label: Moralitas: The Paddock and the Mouse
contentParts.summary: The concluding morality warns against false fellowship and allegorizes body, soul, world, and sudden death.

notes: The range is deliberately conservative. The repository XML's unsupported legacy date 1450 is not adopted; OTA's 1480-1489 record is useful but not by itself conclusive.

dateStatements.display: Fragments survive in witnesses written or augmented in the early sixteenth century
notes: Makculloch preserves the prologue and Cock and Jasp material; Asloan preserves The Two Mice. This is a witness date, not a composition date.

dateStatements.display: The earliest surviving edition has a colophon dated 16 December 1569 and a title page dated 1570
notes: Smith speculated about a lost earlier Chepman and Myllar printing but stated that evidence was entirely wanting; it is not promoted to an event here.

extent.description: The XML represents 13 fable narratives, 13 moralities, and 2 prologues: 28 intellectual sections containing 424 stanza groups and 2,975 line elements. Of these, 2,968 lines carry inherited numbers; a seven-line stanza in the Cock and Fox Moralitas is unnumbered. Two numbered lines sit directly outside LG. The intellectual allocation is 21 prologue stanzas/147 lines, 324 narrative stanzas/2,268 lines, and 79 morality stanzas/560 lines.

formStatements.description: The work is stanzaic verse, predominantly seven-line rhyme royal (ababbcc); the encoded base also contains seven eight-line stanzas.

scope.description: short maxims, names, and embedded learned phrases scattered through the work

notes: Henryson's authorship is modeled separately from Aesop's traditional authority and from the modern editor, transcribers, printers, scribes, encoders, and repositories.
The preferred date is late fifteenth century, perhaps 1470s-1480s. The XML's 1450 and OTA's 1480-1489 are legacy metadata points, not interchangeable certainties.
Dunfermline is a well-supported associated place for Henryson, while Fife as a place of composition remains tentative.
The 2,975-line total is an XML count: 2,968 inherited numbered lines plus one unnumbered seven-line stanza. It should not be mistaken for a newly established canonical lineation.
The XML's second DIV2 labeled prologue inside The Lion and the Mouse is the 168-line narrative; this manifest repairs the intellectual labeling without modifying the source.

Complete source-object caches are recorded in lineage: Bassandyne's unique print, Smith's full reprint volume, the 454-canvas Bannatyne main manuscript, and the 628-canvas surviving Asloan manuscript. Their work portions and historical losses remain explicit.

No reusable source-evaluation or modeling problem was found beyond the project skill's existing rules on mixed electronic bases, provider-relative completeness, witness/transcript separation, discontinuous work portions, and rights-layer separation.

next_steps: Prefer a current specialist argument grounded in internal allusions, Henryson's documented career, and witness chronology before narrowing either range or place.
openQuestions.question: Can the work's composition be dated more tightly than the late fifteenth century or securely localized to Dunfermline/Fife?

next_steps: Retain the represented 13-fable sequence as the cataloging unit while tracking new stemmatic or codicological scholarship on independent circulation, witness order, and print editorial intervention.
notes: content_structure_status complete means all sections of the represented sequence are enumerated, not that the unity debate is resolved.
openQuestions.question: Do the thirteen fables constitute one authorially unified and ordered collection, or does the familiar sequence partly reflect early print transmission and editorial compilation?

next_steps: Locate OSTA/DOST production notes or perform a systematic machine collation against all five Smith streams and the Bassandyne facsimile.
notes: The current evidence combines a BASS PR label and Bassandyne title, Harleian odd-page markers, and a Charteris-only unnumbered stanza.
openQuestions.question: Which Smith stream supplied each reading in OTA 0243 and the U-M continuous text?

next_steps: Model individual analogue relations only when supported by fable-specific textual comparison; do not turn broad Aesopic affiliation into direct physical lineage.
openQuestions.question: Which exact Latin and vernacular exemplars did Henryson use for each individual fable?

notes: Henryson is documented as a notary and teacher at Dunfermline in 1477-1478. This association is not automatically a place-of-composition claim.
OTA's legacy code gives ?FIFE, and Henryson's Dunfermline association makes Fife plausible, but no direct composition evidence was found.

The attribution is sustained across early witnesses and prints and accepted by current scholarship. Aesop is a named traditional authority and source association, not a coauthor of the Middle Scots work.
Smith edited the five early source streams printed in the edition from which the electronic lineage descends.

scope.description: 1906 printed source representation, not medieval authorship

notes: Smith states that Stevenson transcribed and collated all five texts.

scope.description: source transcription and collation for Smith's 1906 edition

subjects.label: Animals as human social and moral figures
Social estates, law, exploitation, and institutional corruption

summaries.value: A sequence of thirteen Middle Scots beast fables in which animal narratives open into explicit moral interpretation. Henryson combines Aesopic storytelling with satire, theology, legal and social criticism, and reflection on prudence, power, poverty, justice, and human appetite. A general prologue explains the value of mingling serious doctrine with merry fiction, and a second prologue before The Lion and the Mouse stages an encounter with Aesop.

The represented order is: general prologue; Cock and Jasp; Two Mice; Cock and Fox; Fox's Confession to Friar Wolf; Trial or Parliament of Beasts; Sheep and Dog; second prologue and Lion and Mouse; Preaching of the Swallow; Fox, Wolf, and Cadger; Fox, Wolf, and Shadow of the Moon; Wolf and Wether; Wolf and Lamb; and Paddock and Mouse. Every fable narrative is followed by a Moralitas.

The repository representation is one continuous 2,975-line text whose 109 page markers follow Smith's Harleian odd pages 3-219, but its title is Bassandyne 1571 and at least one unnumbered stanza comes from a stream absent in Harleian. Counts describe the XML and its inherited lineation, not a new critical edition.

notes: The spelling follows the established early-print and encoded title tradition; it is not claimed as an authorial autograph title.
titles.value: The Morall Fabillis of Esope the Phrygian

work.author: Robert Henryson
work.displayTitle: The Morall Fabillis of Esope the Phrygian
work.editor: G. Gregory Smith (1906 source edition); George Stevenson transcribed and collated its five source streams
work.preferredTitle: The Morall Fabillis of Esope the Phrygian

publication.embeddedTitle: Robert Henryson's The morall fabillis of Esope the Phrygian — The Morall Fabillis of Esope the Phrygian, compylit in eloquent, & ornate scottis meter, be M. Robert Henrisone, scolmaister of Dunfermling. Newlie corectit, and vendicat, fra mony errouris, quhilkis war ouer sene in the last prenting, quhair baith Lynes, and haill versis war left owt. Inprinted att Edinburgh be me Thomas Bassandyne, dwelland at the nether bow (anno.) 1571

work.regionDisplay: Scotland; Henryson is associated with Dunfermline, but the exact place of composition is unproved

subjects.label: Animals as human social and moral figures
Social estates, law, exploitation, and institutional corruption

work.summary: A sequence of thirteen Middle Scots beast fables in which animal narratives open into explicit moral interpretation. Henryson combines Aesopic storytelling with satire, theology, legal and social criticism, and reflection on prudence, power, poverty, justice, and human appetite. A general prologue explains the value of mingling serious doctrine with merry fiction, and a second prologue before The Lion and the Mouse stages an encounter with Aesop.
